content.full_text:
Re
Farn PourrcAt- PRACTTcES CoiuurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
March 5,20A4
Karin D. Troedsson, Asst. City Attorney
City of St. Helena
1480 Main Street
St. Helena, CA94574
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-03-285
Dear Ms. Troedsson:
This letter is in response to your request on behalfof Councilmember Eric Sklar
for assistance regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the 'Act").I Because your letter is general in n-ature and relates to no specific
governmental decision, we are providing informal assistance.'
QUBSTTONS
l. May Councilmember Sklarparticipate in decisions regarding approval of specific
affordable housing projects in the City of St. Helena (either at the task force or
council level) or on the county level (either through his involvement with the
Board or NVHA)? How does this answer change ifthe project is proposed by
}N/CH or other unrelated developers?
2. May Councilmernber Sklar participate in decisions regarding general affordable
housing policies in the City of St. Helena (either at the task force or council level)
or on the county level (either through his involvement with the Board or NVHA)?
These policies may include prioritizing water and sewer resources, creation of and
allocation of funds from the housing trust fund, and adoption of the inclusionary
ordinance.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Reg. 18329, subd. (c), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-03-285
Page No. 2
3. If Councilmember Sklar's spouse were to glveup her salary and work for free for
the NVCH, would it change the analysis? If her current salary is diverted to a
Sklar Family Foundation, a nonprofit, would it change the analpis? What if her
salary was diverted to a charitable foundation in which she maintained no
involvement or control?
CONCLUSIONS
1. and 2. The application ofthe conflict-oflinterest rules is a fact-driven one.
Councilmember Sklar may not make, participate in making or influence a decision
regarding an application filed by NVCH. Whether Mr. Sklar will face a conflict of
interest in the other tlpes of decisions will depend on whether sources of income to him
initiateproceedings before the governmental bodies on which he sits and whether a
material financial effect will result on those sources ofincome as a consequence of his
participation in the decision-making process.
3. MI/CH will continue to be a source of income to the Sklar family for l}months
following the last salary received by Mrs. SHar. Regarding other recipiemts of her
income, the primary focus would be whether the income from NVCH which N&s. Sklar
would otherurise receive directly, would wind up indirectly in the pockets of the Sklar
family.
FACTS
The City of St. Helena is a small city in the Napa Valley of approximately 6,000
people. Pursuant to a general plan growth managerne,nt policy, the city only issues 17
building permits for market rate housing per year, although affordable rmits are exempt.
The city also has a limited supply of land that can be developed. The city's housing
element has been approved by the California Deparhne,nt of Housing and Community
Development.
The city has limited water and sewer resources, and there is discussion at the
council level regarding prioritizing these limitd resources with affordable housing as a
priority category. The council will also be considering specific policies regarding
affordable housing in the near future including an inclusionary ordinance.
Councilmember Sklm has recently joined a for-profit corporation that builds
housing. They intend to sell the housing forbelow mmket rates, some of which will
qualifr as "affordable housing" under state law. The corporation has two potential
projects currently, one in the City of Napa and one in the City ofAmerican Canyon, both
of which are located in Napa County. The corporation does not anticipate projects in the
City of St. Helena. The corporation is not seeking any public funds for its projects.
Councilmernber Sklar draws a salary from the corporation in excess of $500 per year.


File No. I-03-285
Page No. 3
Councilmember Sklar is also a voting mernber or alternate to three other
governmental bodies dealing with affordable housing issues.
1) The first body is the St. Helena Housing Task Forcg an advisory body to the
city council that develops housingpolicies, including the housing element and the
inclusionary ordinance.
2) The Napa Valley Housing Authority (NVHA) is the second body in which
Councilmember Sklar participates. The NVHA operates under a joint powers agreement
between the various cities in the county, and the County of Napa and provides seasonal
farm worker housing administers the Napa County Affordable Housing Trust Fund and
monitors afi[ordable housing developments.
3) The third body is the Napa Valley Community Affordable Housing Advisory
Board @oard). The member jurisdictions of the Board include the County ofNap4 the
City of St. Helena, the City of Napa, the Town of Yountville and the Napa Valley
Housing Authority. The Board's duties include: developing policies and plans for
affordable housing; prioritizing the use of housing trust funds; soliciting proposals for the
use of the housing trust funds; review, analysis and recommendation of appropriation of
the housing trust funds and housing credits; creation of a comprehensive application
package, rating and ranking criteria and notices of funding availability and review,
evaluation and recommendations regarding adoption ofnew ordinances or changes to
existing ordinances affecting housing trust funds ofthe memberjurisdictions.
Counsilmerrber Sklar's spouse is employed as a project manager ofNapa Valley
Community Housing OIVCH), a nonprofit affordable housing group located in the City
of Napa. She earns more than $500 a year in her positiorq and is involved in affordable
housing projects tlroughout Napa county, including the city of St. Hele,na
Noble House Construction and Development is a housing development company
in St. Helena. The company has askd Councilmexnber Sklar to recuse himself from
consideration of all affordable housing prqects and goveming policies.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govenrmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest
(regulation 18700O)(l) - (8)), which is discussed below.
l. & 2. fs Councilmember Sklar a public official making participating in making
or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As a member
of the St. Helena City Council, Mr. Sklar is a public ofEcial. (Section 82048; regulation
18701(a).) As a council member, unless disqualified underthe conflict-oiinterest


File No. I-03-285
Page No. 4
provisions of the Act, Mr. Sklar will makg participate in making and influence
govemmental decisions.3
In this latter regard, when a public official votes on a mattetr, he or she is "making
a govenrmental decision." (Reg.18702.1, subd. (a)(1).) Similmly, when apublic official
prepares or presents any report, analysis, or opinion, either orally or in writing which
requires the exercise of the official and thepurpose ofwhich is to influence a
govemmental decision, the official is participating in making a govemmental decision
(regulation 18702.2(b)(2). Thus, should Mr. Sklar vote with the council, task force,
NVHA or Board in the capacrty delineated above, he will be making a governmental
decision. Should he participate in the deliberations on these bodies in connection with a
vote, he will be participating in making a govemmental decision.
3. What are Councilmember Sklar's economic interests?
Section 871A3 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immdiate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
o
o
A public official has an economic interest in a business e,ntity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment a of $2,000 ormore (section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officetr, parhrer, kustee,
employeg or holds any position of management (section 87 I 03(d); regulation
18703.r(b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 ormore (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);
3 If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 C'87200 filers- irclude members of city
councils) and he or she has a conflict ofinterest in a decision noticed at a public meetiog, then he or she
must (l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identiff each 6rpe of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details ofthe economic interes! as discussed in regulation
18702.5(bX1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, coreerot calendars, abserces and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal inierests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. ($ 87105). Since Mr. SHar is a member of the St. Helena City Council,
these requirements apply to him.
a An indirect inveshnent or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the offrcial's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediati family''is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


a
a
File No. I-03-285
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 871 03 (c); regulation I 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months pnor to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 $.4;
A public official has an economic interest in his or herpersonal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family - this is the'frcrsonal financial
effects" rule (section 871A3; regulation 18703.5).
Appllng these factors to the facts you present, Mr. Sklar has an economic
interest in the corporation that employs him. The second financial interest pertinent to
your question would be Mr. Sklar's community property interest in the income of his
spouse, who is employed by NVCH. A public official's communityproperty interest in a
spouse's income is considered income to thepublic official. (g 82030, subd. (a).) This
means that thepublic official has an economic interest in the souroe ofthat income and
may lead to the public official being potentially disqualified from making decisions
regarding the source ofthe income. ($$ 87100 and 87103, subd. (c).) Thereforg Mr.
Sklar also has an economic interest in NVCH.s
You pose the question as to whether it would make a difference if NIrs. Sklar
worked for free for NVCH or otherurise diverted her salary to the Sklar Family
Foundation or a charitable foundation in which she maintained no involverrent or
control. Assuming for the moment, that any of these altematives were taken, NVCH
would continue to be an economic interest of lv1r. Sklar's for l}mont]rs after last
receiving income from NVCH through his spouse. Thus, anyalternative arrangement, to
the exte,nt it would succeed in eliminating income to the Sklarfamily, would have a
delayed impact.
Regarding the specific options you propose, you provide no infomration on which
an analysis can be performed regarding the specific provisions of a hypothetical
foundation or charitable organization. The primary focus, of course, would be whether
the income from NVCH that Mrs. Sklar would otherurise receive directly would wind up
indirectly in the pockets ofthe Sklar farnily.
4. & 5.: Are Mr. Sklar's economic interests direcfly or indirectty involved in the
governmental decisions, and would they be materially affected by a dmision?
As indicated above, your letter poses no specific govemmental decisions at issue
but instead refers generally to two groups of decisions: 1) decisions regarding affordable
5 As Mr. Sklar's economic interest is founded on principles of community property, the income
threshold to Mrs. Sklar would be $1,000, of which $500 would bi attributed to Mr. Skhr. ihough your
letter indicates Mrs. Sklar's eams more than $500 per year as a project manager for NVCH, we shall
assume for the purposes of this letter that she earns more than $1,000.


File No. I-03-285
Page No. 6
housing projects in the City of St. Helena, and2) decisions regarding affordable housing
policies in the City of St. Helena. In your letter, you ask us to assume that Mr. Sklar has
a conflict of interest in the first group of decisions where the projects are proposed by Mr.
Sklar's employer.
In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the govemmental decision
(regulation 18704). An official's economic interesf including sources of income, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency whe,n that party: "[i]nitiates the
proceeding in which the decision will be madeby filing an application, claim, appeal, or
similar request or; Q) [i]s a named party in, or the subject o{, a proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding
if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or conkact with, the subject petrson." (Reg. lS7}aJ@).)
Thus, whether a given source of income to Mr. Sklarhas proposed a given decision is
critical when determining whether a conflict of interest may result.
Forinstance, anyreasonably foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest
that is a source of income to a public official, and which is directly involved in a decision
before the official's agency, is deemed material (regulation 18705.3(a)). In contrast, if a
source of income is not "directly involved" in the governmental decision, in order to
determine whether or not any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on that economic
interest is material, the materiality standards applicable to nonprofit entities, such as
N]y'CH, set forth in regulation 18705.3, subdivision OX2) would apply. (Copy enclosed.)
If the source of income is a business entity, the standards of regulation I 8705. I ,
subdivision (c), would apply. (Copy enclosed.) Each decision, regardless of whether it
goes to policy or a specific project, must be amlyzdunder this framework.
6. Is the material financial effect reasonably foreseeable?
As noted abovg a conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably
foreseeable financial effects of a govemmental decision on apublic official's eoonomic
interests is material. As discussed abovg where the source of income is directly involved
in the governmental decision, the financial effect is presurned material. (Reg. 18700(a).)
An effect upon economic interests is considered 'teasonably foreseeabld'if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Ree. 18706.,suM. (a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. l9S.)
Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
efiFect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic
interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the agency's
decision. It is important to remernber that once an official is determined to have a
conflict of interest in a decision by virtue of a given economic interest, it is irrelevant that
a diflerent economic interest does not create a conflict of interest.


File No. l-03-285
Page No. 7
7. & 8. Exceptions.
An official who might otherurise have a conflict of interest in a particular decision
may still participate in that decision if the circumstances are such that an exception to the
conflict-of-interest rules might apply. The'lublic generally''exception maybe invoked
when the financial effect of a decision upon apublic official's economic interests is not
distinguishable from the effect of the decision on a "significant segm€,nt" of the public
generally. (Section 87103; regulation 18707(a).) Your account of the facts does not include
information on particular decisions in which the "public generally'' exception need be
considered.
An official with a conflict of interest may still participate in the decision under the
"legally required participation" exception. This is an exception that tlpically applies when
an agency is unable to asserrble a quorum of its me,rrrbers without participation of an
official who has a conflict of interest. Again, you have not suggested that this exception
need be considered at present.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: C. Scott Tocher
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
CSTjg

ar

An effect upon economic interests is considered 'teasonably foreseeabld'if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Ree. 18706.,suM. (a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. l9S.)
Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
efiFect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic
interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the agency's
decision. It is important to remernber that once an official is determined to have a
conflict of interest in a decision by virtue of a given economic interest, it is irrelevant that
a diflerent economic interest does not create a conflict of interest.


File No. l-03-285
7. & 8. Exceptions.
An official who might otherurise have a conflict of interest in a particular decision
may still participate in that decision if the circumstances are such that an exception to the
conflict-of-interest rules might apply. The'lublic generally''exception maybe invoked
when the financial effect of a decision upon apublic official's economic interests is not
distinguishable from the effect of the decision on a "significant segm€,nt" of the public
generally. (Section 87103; regulation 18707(a).) Your account of the facts does not include
information on particular decisions in which the "public generally'' exception need be
considered.
An official with a conflict of interest may still participate in the decision under the
"legally required participation" exception. This is an exception that tlpically applies when
an agency is unable to asserrble a quorum of its me,rrrbers without participation of an
official who has a conflict of interest. Again, you have not suggested that this exception
need be considered at present.

QUBSTTONS l.
